name: Gorgons Extended
description: The Gorgons, fearsome daughters of ancient sea deities, are monstrous figures in Greek mythology. While often remembered for Medusa, the only mortal Gorgon, the original Gorgons were Stheno and Euryale, immortal sisters of terrifying power. They are typically depicted as winged women with venomous snakes for hair, sharp claws, and bronze hands. Their gaze is the most potent weapon, capable of instantly turning any living creature to stone. Beyond petrification, the Gorgons possess immense strength and resilience, making them formidable opponents. Their origins are shrouded in mystery, with some accounts tracing their lineage to Phorcys and Ceto, primordial sea deities, while others suggest they were daughters of Typhon and Echidna. The Gorgons represent primal fear, untamed nature, and the dangers lurking beyond the familiar world. Their existence serves as a reminder of the power of the ancient gods and the monstrous beings that roamed the earth in the age of heroes. The Gorgons were not always monstrous; some accounts suggest that Medusa was once a beautiful priestess of Athena, cursed for her vanity or for being violated by Poseidon in Athena's temple. This tragic backstory adds a layer of complexity to the Gorgon myth, highlighting themes of divine retribution and the consequences of hubris. The Gorgons, especially Medusa, have become enduring symbols of female rage, power, and transformation, inspiring countless works of art and literature throughout history.
subtitle: Petrifying sisters of ancient myth
appearance: Gorgons are typically depicted as winged women with snakes for hair, sharp claws, bronze hands, and sometimes tusks. Their faces are often described as hideous and terrifying, with wide eyes and a gaping mouth. Some depictions include scales or serpent-like features on their bodies.
habitat: Isolated islands, remote caves, and other desolate locations far from civilization. They often guard entrances to the underworld or other dangerous realms.
behavior: Aggressive, territorial, and hostile towards intruders. They are driven by a primal instinct to protect their territory and punish those who dare to trespass. Some accounts suggest they are also driven by a desire for revenge against those who wronged them or their kin.

keyMyths.title: Perseus and Medusa
keyMyths.description: Perseus, aided by Athena and Hermes, slays Medusa by using a mirrored shield to avoid her gaze and decapitating her. He later uses her head as a weapon.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: Provides an early account of the Gorgons' parentage and their role in Greek mythology.
sources.text: Provides an early account of the Gorgons' parentage and their role in Greek mythology.

sources.author: Ovid
sources.description: Offers a detailed description of Medusa's transformation and her encounter with Perseus.
